text: The surgeon is dissecting the left lower retrorectus space, separating retrorectus fat from the attenuated posterior rectus sheath below the arcuate line. The fibers of the posterior rectus sheath are visible, extending to the midline. An incision is made through the posterior rectus sheath to enter the preperitoneal space. The surgeon uses the XI robot for dissection, employing bipolar cautery to manage vessels, and prepares to cross over after fully exposing the linea alba.

The surgeon crosses over to the opposite side of the retrorectus space, using scissors to make a cold incision without energy, revealing preperitoneal fat. A cuff of posterior rectus sheath is left on the linea alba to prevent weakening and potential hernia formation. The surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection, carefully bringing down visible preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon carefully approaches the hernia area, using cautery on the ventral side of the posterior rectus sheath to manage vessels without risking thermal injury to the bowel. The dissection reveals a thin layer of fat, which is left intact to avoid damaging the diastatic fascia. Hemostasis is maintained to ensure clear visibility of tissue types, crucial for distinguishing bowel from other structures during the crossover.

The surgeon continues the crossover into the preperitoneal space, using a cold cut technique to score the ventral side of the posterior rectus sheath, minimizing bleeding. The scissor is inserted behind the posterior rectus sheath to create distance from the peritoneum, ensuring no bowel is adhered. The posterior rectus sheath is placed under tension by the surgeon's left hand, allowing for potential scoring without cutting. The procedure involves a bilateral TAR, with careful consideration of preserving the hernia sac.

The surgeon navigates the lower abdomen where the second larger hernia is located, observing thin peritoneum and scar tissue around the intra-abdominal space. The rectus muscle serves as a landmark, with efforts to maintain the integrity of the peritoneum and avoid damaging the anterior rectus sheath. Bloodstained tissue is visible, indicating previous bleeding, and the surgeon carefully dissects to find areolar tissue. The left superior edge of the hernia is exposed, and the surgeon attempts to bring down the hernia sac, noting the difficulty due to scar tissue.

The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity by pushing and scraping with scissors, breaking through the peritoneum at the edge of the hernia defect. The view inside the cavity reveals minimal adhesions, making it difficult to take down the hernia sac. The surgeon divides the peritoneum along the neck of the hernia, exposing a larger defect on the right side and a smaller defect in the suprapubic area on the left. The anterior fascia appears as thicker white tissue, which the surgeon attempts to preserve while moving towards the caudal edge of the hernia defect, aiming to stay in the preperitoneal or right retro-rectus plane.

The surgeon navigates between two distinct planes, opting for the deeper plane to access the space between the retro-rectus fat and the attenuated posterior rectus sheath. The sheath is incised, revealing the true space of Retzius. The operation proceeds efficiently with good hemostasis, allowing clear visualization of anatomical landmarks such as the right superior pubic rami. The surgeon retracts tissue to identify the cotton candy-like areolar tissue, indicating a suitable dissection plane. The procedure continues along the right edge of the defect, below the arcuate line, where the posterior rectus sheath is less distinct. The surgeon opens the area broadly, observing muscle twitching, and confirms entry into the retro-rectus space by incising the attenuated posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon is bringing down the posterior rectus sheath, distinguishing between the retrorectus space on the right side and the preperitoneal space on the left side. The attenuated transverse salus is being cut, and the surgeon is focusing on maintaining hemostasis while performing dissection in the retropubic space. The procedure involves opening the contralateral posterior rectus sheath and developing the retrorectus space on the contralateral side.

The surgeon navigates through scarred tissue, using their left hand to expose areolar tissue while incising the peritoneum. They maintain a straight line from the smaller lower hernia to the wider upper hernia, avoiding close dissection near the lower fascial edge. The surgeon carefully progresses from caudal to cephalad, mindful of the inferior epigastric vessels, and guides the scissors between the posterior rectus sheath and the rectus muscle.

The surgeon carefully dissects in the retrorectus space, staying approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba. The posterior rectus sheath is visibly retracted, and the surgeon follows the edge of the fat to maintain the neoperitoneum on the anterior rectus sheath. The preperitoneal space is developed on the right side of the screen, while the retrorectus space is shown being dissected. The surgeon aims to connect these spaces by extending the preperitoneal space across the contralateral linea alba.

The surgeon cuts the posterior rectus sheath to join the preperitoneal space on the right with the retrorectus space on the left, creating a single flap. The peritoneum is close to the line of division, prompting further preperitoneal dissection to avoid perforation. The surgeon clears preperitoneal fat and transverse salus fascia, using TAP skills to facilitate the dissection. The right hand is restricted, requiring assistance to adjust the flex joint for increased range. The left hand pushes down the posterior rectus sheath, revealing areolar tissue for easier dissection. The surgeon incises the left posterior rectus sheath, maintaining hemostasis to prepare for later TAR procedure.

The surgeon completes the crossover and epigastric subxiphoid dissection by scraping the posterior rectus sheath to remove transverse salus tissue, revealing a shiny posterior rectus sheath. The left hand guides the scissor division of the posterior rectus sheath, maintaining a distance of about a centimeter from the linea alba. The retrorectus space is opened by lifting fibro fatty areolar tissue, and the bowel appears insufflated due to CO₂ entry during initial port placement.
